coredata.dc:title: A high-performance zinc anode enabled by carbon quantum dots with H2O-structure-regulating capabilities
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Zhao, Zhiwei
Yang, Zhi
Chen, Yazhou
Zhong, Guiming
Guo, Limin
Peng, Zhangquan
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous Zn metal batteries offer high specific capacity and adequate safety, but suffer from limited stability and longevity primarily due to interfacial H2O-induced parasitic reactions on Zn anode. It thus is imperative to understand and control the interfacial H2O behavior for enhancing electrochemical performance of Zn anode. Herein, we formulate a functional aqueous Zn2+ electrolyte, containing well-dispersed Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (ZCDs) that can dynamically self-enrich on the Zn anode surface and thus endowed with unique interfacial “H2O-structure-regulating” capabilities. The Zn anode in contact with this exotic electrolyte demonstrates an essentially dendrite-free plating/stripping process, a 64.1% HER inhibition efficiency, a substantially prolonged cycle life (over 1500 h at 5 mA cm−2), an exceptional cumulative capacity of ∼3.75 Ah cm−2, and a capacity retention of a Zn||V2O5 battery up to ∼83% over 4000 cycles. A mechanistic study, using a combination of in situ spectroscopies and density functional theory calculations, provides direct spectroscopic evidence that the enriched ZCDs on the Zn surface reduce the proportion of reactive solvated H2O and thereof suppresses the H2O-induced parasitic reactions. The results reported here underscore the crucial significance of regulating the interfacial H2O structures on Zn2+ deposition process, and also provide inspirational design criteria for better Zn-based energy conversion and storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc anode
Interfacial water
Carbon quantum dot
In situ spectroscopy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22102183 , 22202202 , 22232005 , 22242009 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2022M713091 ), National Key R&D Program of China (Project No. 2022YFA1504002 ), "Scientist Studio Funding" from Tianmu Lake Institute of Advanced Energy Storage Technologies Co., Ltd., and Dalian supports high-level talent innovation and entrepreneurship projects (no. 2021RD14 ).

The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22102183, 22202202, 22232005, 22242009), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M713091), National Key R&D Program of China (Project No. 2022YFA1504002), "Scientist Studio Funding" from Tianmu Lake Institute of Advanced Energy Storage Technologies Co. Ltd. and Dalian supports high-level talent innovation and entrepreneurship projects (no. 2021RD14).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of interfacial reactions of Zn anode in (a) conventional Zn2+ electrolyte and (b) functional Zn2+ electrolyte containing ZCDs. BE: benchmark ZnSO4 electrolyte, ISE: inorganic nanoparticle suspension electrolyte.

(a) Long-cycling performance of symmetric Zn||Zn cells. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) curves. (c) Laser confocal scanning microscope images of Zn electrode after cycling at 5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 2.5 mAh cm−2. (d) Coulombic efficiencies of asymmetric Zn||Ti cells in BE and ISE; Insets are the corresponding voltage profiles of selected cycles. (e) XRD results for the Zn deposits on Ti and Zn electrode surfaces after cycling. (f) In situ optical microscopy photos of Zn plating behavior in a transparent cell operated at 5.0 mA cm−2. (g) The Tafel plots tested in BE and ISE using three-electrodes system. (h) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of Zn electrode in three-electrodes device at 2 mV s − 1.

(a) DEMS profiles of Zn/Zn symmetric cells during charge-discharge states at 0.5 mA/cm2. (b) The evolution amount of hydrogen in different working steps. Three-dimensional in situ Raman spectra of (c) BE and (d) ISE at different voltages during the entire charge-discharge states at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. (e) Raman intensity change mapping during Zn2+ deposition under a constant current of 5 mA cm−2 in BE (left) and ISE (right).

(a) Lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of different H2O structures obtained by DFT calculations. (b) Adsorption energies comparison of different H2O structures on (002) lattice plane of Zn, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups of ZCDs, respectively.

The evolution of ATR-SEIRAS spectra as function of potential for the (a) BE and (b) ISE system. Comparative selected ATR-SEIRAS spectra from (c) −0.55 V to −1.05 and (d) from −1.065 V to −1.2 V. (e) Potential-dependent population of the OH stretching modes of interfacial H2O structures.
(a) Rate capability and (b) long-term cycling of the Zn||AC hybrid supercapacitors. (c) Rate capability and (d) long-term cycling performances of Zn||V2O5 batteries.

head.ce:title: A high-performance zinc anode enabled by carbon quantum dots with H2O-structure-regulating capabilities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dTianmu Lake Institute of Advanced Energy Storage Technologies Co. Ltd., Liyang 213300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous Zn metal batteries offer high specific capacity and adequate safety, but suffer from limited stability and longevity primarily due to interfacial H2O-induced parasitic reactions on Zn anode. It thus is imperative to understand and control the interfacial H2O behavior for enhancing electrochemical performance of Zn anode. Herein, we formulate a functional aqueous Zn2+ electrolyte, containing well-dispersed Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (ZCDs) that can dynamically self-enrich on the Zn anode surface and thus endowed with unique interfacial “H2O-structure-regulating” capabilities. The Zn anode in contact with this exotic electrolyte demonstrates an essentially dendrite-free plating/stripping process, a 64.1% HER inhibition efficiency, a substantially prolonged cycle life (over 1500 h at 5 mA cm−2), an exceptional cumulative capacity of ∼3.75 Ah cm−2, and a capacity retention of a Zn||V2O5 battery up to ∼83% over 4000 cycles. A mechanistic study, using a combination of in situ spectroscopies and density functional theory calculations, provides direct spectroscopic evidence that the enriched ZCDs on the Zn surface reduce the proportion of reactive solvated H2O and thereof suppresses the H2O-induced parasitic reactions. The results reported here underscore the crucial significance of regulating the interfacial H2O structures on Zn2+ deposition process, and also provide inspirational design criteria for better Zn-based energy conversion and storage devices.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The soaring increase in energy consumption of the modern society calls for safe, economical, efficient and green energy conversion and storage technologies, particularly for grid-scale applications [1,2]. As alternatives and/or being complementary to the ubiquitous lithium-ion batteries, rechargeable aqueous Zn metal batteries represent a promising solution in terms of more balanced kinetics and higher stability than some other contenders of K, Mg, Ca and Al-based batteries [3–5].

Nevertheless, the Zn anode is currently being plagued by multiple challenges, such as competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), aqueous electrolyte corrosion and uncontrollable dendrite formation, resulting in low Coulombic efficiency, limited lifespan and hindered practical deployment [6]. It is well recognized that the solid electrolyte interphase, formed by the decomposition of aprotic electrolytes on the anodes in lithium-based batteries, is absent on Zn anode immersed in conventional aqueous solution [7]. Therefore, the reactive H2O molecules in the aqueous electrolyte can corrode Zn anode and/or electrochemically decompose at the potentials required for the operation of Zn2+ plating/stripping, in which the formed parasitic products exacerbate the unevenness of electric field distribution on Zn surface resulting in uncontrollable dendrite growth. In turn, the loose and porous structure of dendrites increases the contact area between Zn anode and aqueous electrolyte, providing a surplus of active sites for undesirable HER and corrosion reactions. Therefore, these challenges are, to certain extent, mainly related to the H2O-induced parasitic reactions [8], that is, interfacial H2O performs an essential role for stability and reversibility of Zn anode.

Over recent years, substantial pioneering works have been targeted towards eliminating or minimizing the reactivity of interfacial H2O molecules to boost electrochemical performance of Zn anode, including protective layers (e.g., graphene, metal, metallic oxide, and artificial solid electrolyte interphase) [8–10], ‘water-in-salt’ electrolytes [11], functional organic additives [12] and so on. Nevertheless, the structure, composition, and dynamic evolution of interfacial H2O in regulating the reversibility and stability of Zn anode, remain somewhat elusive and require more detailed interpretation. Regarding that, the majority of reported reaction mechanisms have been suggested by ectopic analyses and theoretical simulations. To facilitate fundamental understandings and further control strategies for H2O-induced parasitic reactions, it is imperative to obtain the direct spectroscopic evidence via advanced in situ characterization techniques.

In this study, we report an effective “H2O-sturcure-regulating” strategy to suppress the H2O-induced parasitic reactions of Zn anode frequently occurring in the benchmark ZnSO4 electrolyte (BE) (Fig. 1 a). This strategy is realized by an inorganic nanoparticle suspension electrolyte (ISE) that consists of Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (ZCDs) dispersed in the conventional BE. By a combination of in situ attenuated total reflection surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we uncover the structure, composition, and dynamic evolution of interfacial H2O in the BE and ISE. It is found that the reactive solvated H2O are responsible for the majority of parasitic reactions of Zn anode surface. The rationally designed ZCDs can dynamically self-enrich on the Zn anode surface and serve as the regulatory site to reduce the proportion of isolated H2O and solvated H2O (Fig. 1b). As a result, this peculiar electrolyte significantly suppresses the H2O-induced interfacial parasitic reactions as demonstrated by differential electrochemical mass spectrometry (DEMS) and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). By virtue of this regard, the Zn anode presents an essentially dendrite-free Zn plating/stripping process and enhanced longevity. The mechanistic understanding and corresponding strategies reported here provide crucial insights and enlightenments that will aid in the design of more versatile Zn anode/electrolyte interface required for better Zn-based devices.

The ZCDs were prepared by a low temperature calcination reaction (synthesis details shown in the Supporting Information). The obtained ZCDs exhibit a monodisperse quasi-spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.24 nm (Fig. S1a), whose lattice fringe of 0.21 nm corresponds to the stacking distance of graphene layers in graphitic carbons (100) [13]. A broad XRD peak centered at 20° (Fig. S1b) corroborates the partial graphitization of the ZCDs. The ZCDs’ surface is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties, that have been confirmed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA)/differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Fig. S1c), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. S1d) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S2). These functional groups are hydrophilic and confer great dispersibility on ZCDs in BE [14].

The electrochemical performance of Zn anode in ISE, after obtaining the optimized concentration of 1.0 g/L (Fig. S3a-c), was first evaluated by the galvanostatic cycling of symmetric Zn||Zn and asymmetric Zn||Ti cells. The voltage-time profile of the symmetric Zn||Zn cells with ISE exhibits dramatically prolonged cycle life at both 2 mA cm−2 and 5 mA cm−2 with no fluctuations in overpotential of the last ten cycles, presenting a competitive advantage over most of previous works (Table S3). Conversely, the BE group quickly fails due to shorting within the initial cycles (Fig. 2 a and S3d). Immediate EIS after 1500 stripping/plating cycles was also carried out to check the occurrence of the small localized electrical connection. A slight increase in charge transfer resistance (Rct) reflected by high frequency semicircle ensures the accuracy of the life-span (Fig. 2b) [15]. Remarkably, the three-dimensional depth distribution images of the cycled Zn anodes manifest vertical dendrite growth of Zn in BE with higher average height (6.8 μm) than that in ISE (3.5 μm), using ex situ laser confocal scanning microscope (Fig. 2c). The cycled Zn anode after 20 cycles in ISE displays a compact morphology, while irregular Zn deposits and adherent glass fiber are detected on the Zn foil in BE (Fig. S4a and b). After switching to the Zn||Ti cell, the cell in ISE still maintains a Coulombic efficiency of 98.92% up to 500 cycles, far superior to the one that is short circuited after 78h in BE (Fig. 2d). Chronoamperometry of Zn||Zn cells (Fig. S5) shows that Zn2+ plating in ISE has a shorter time for 2D diffusion regime and a limited tendency for the current to level off, indicating a homogeneous spatial distribution of growth centers. Cyclic voltammetry of Zn||Ti cells (Fig. S6) shows that ISE electrolyte brings about an increased nucleation overpotential and thus promotes the development of smaller nucleus [16]. A preferred (002) orientation of Zn deposits on Ti and Zn (Fig. 2e) is also a direct indicator for improved Zn2+ plating process, which may result from gentle interfacial turbulence and weakened solvated Zn2+ adsorption discussed below [17]. Likewise, in situ optical visualization further displays that a thick and irregular deposition, composed of randomly oriented grains and dendrites, gradually covers the Zn surface during the initial 60 min and finally evolves into ramified dendrites in BE. Conversely, a smooth and clean surface is obtained for Zn in ISE even after plating for 75 min at 5.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. 2f). These results are all attributed to the effects of ZCDs in ISE on the Zn2+ deposition process.

The interfacial parasitic reactions including corrosion and hydrogen evolution on Zn anode surface, were subsequently analyzed using combined electrochemical and in situ spectroscopic techniques. Tafel curve of the Zn electrode recorded in ISE (Fig. 2g) displays a superior anti-corrosion property compared with the case of BE. In addition, the Zn electrode in ISE has a lower exchange current density of 1.0 mA cm–2 than that in BE, which is associated with the adsorption or assembly of ZCDs onto Zn anode surface and also facilitates the mitigation of irreversible Zn2+ depletion. The mitigated corrosion has also been visualized by the results of immersion test (Fig. S7): the Zn foil displays well-maintained surface after immersion in ISE but long-range unevenness and flaky by-product aggregations are observed for Zn in BE. The LSV profiles show that the HER overpotential of Zn in ISE is higher than that of BE (Figs. 2h and S8), revealing the subdued HER activity in ISE. In situ impedance measurements (Fig. S9) also present that the symmetric Zn|ISE|Zn cell maintains a relatively stable Rct during the 20 cycles examined, indicating a stabilized Zn depositing/stripping mode and minor electrolyte consumption compared with in Zn|BE|Zn cell. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) after 20 cycles [18] also presents the lower ECSA value as reflected by the double layer capacitance (Cdl) (5.73 × 10−4 μF cm−2) of Zn in ISE, alluding a limited accessible surface to the electrolyte and thus an excellent interface stability by minimizing the parasitic reactions.

Furthermore, in situ DEMS measurements were employed to quantify the amount of H2 evolved from parasitic corrosion reaction and HER. In Fig. 3 a, it is clearly observed that the H2 spikes in both ISE and BE at open circuit voltage (OCV), upon the connection of the cell to the DEMS analyzer. The instantaneous H2 evolution results from spontaneous corrosion reactions due to the equilibrium potential of Zn2+/Zn being lower than that of H2O/H2. The ISE manifests a much lower gas evolution profile compared with BE at OCV. Apparently, the identical trend holds for charge-discharge process (Fig. 3b and Table S2). The total release of H2 is considerably alleviated in ISE (7.35 μmol) compared to that in BE (20.45 μmol), manifesting 64.1% HER inhibition efficiency during the OCV process and initial charge-discharge cycle. These results clearly indicate that ISE greatly reduces the rate of corrosion reaction and HER on Zn anode. In situ SERS was also employed to clarify the interfacial parasitic reactions. The v 1-SO4 2− mode at 980.7 cm−1 can be used as a probe to detect the concentration dependence of SO4 2− during Zn2+ plating/stripping process over reverse range of −1.2 to 0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 3c and d) [19,20]. Perfectly, the SO4 2− as second coordination sphere species of solvated Zn2+, should remain reversibly weakening and recovering among the Zn2+ plating/stripping process due to electric field force. The ideal trend of v 1-SO4 2 − band is observed during the Zn2+ plating/stripping in ISE. Conversely, the signal in BE almost disappears as the potential decreases to ∼1.10 V and fails to be detectable until a high voltage of ∼−0.75 V. Similar trends of anion depletion in the vicinity of the electrode in BE are also observed under a constant current density of 5 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3e) [21]. Raman mapping also visualizes the 2D distribution of by-products Zn4SO4(OH)6·nH2O (at 965 cm−1) and shows smaller counts within 200–1400 in ISE compared with BE (Fig. S10) [22]. These results suggest that SO4 2− anions are involved in interfacial parasitic reactions to form irreversible passivation layer (e.g., Zn4SO4(OH)6·nH2O) that leads to deteriorated rechargeability in BE.

It is well-established that the interfacial H2O plays a crucial role in many electrochemical reactions, including CO2 electroreduction [23], oxygen reduction reaction [24] and HER [25], in which its structure, composition and dynamic evolution under electrochemical conditions dictate the kinetics and reaction pathways. Naturally, the questions arise as to whether interfacial H2O has a clear correlation with electrochemical performance of Zn anode and how interfacial H2O affects Zn anode stability? On the base of previous works [26,27], the interfacial H2O consists of symmetric H2O with four H bonds, asymmetric H2O (i.e., solvated H2O) with one to three H bonds, and isolated H2O without H bonds. We first carried out DFT calculations (Fig. 4 a) to optimize structure of interfacial H2O and evaluate their relative reactivity [26,27]. It is found that the dissociation trend of interfacial H2O follows the order: solvated H2O (−9.79 eV) > symmetric H2O (−1.093 eV) > the isolated H2O (−0.611 eV). The discrepancy of solvated H2O can be attributed to the interaction of cations with H2O molecules around them, which pulls electrons in the OH bonds toward the O atoms, resulting in more reaction reactivity of solvated H2O. Despite such predictions may be deviations from reality (involving hydrogen bond breaking or more complex steps), we are reasonably sure that solvated H2O will dominate the H2O-induced parasitic reactions on Zn anode surface, that is, regulation of the interfacial H2O structures at electrified interface is expected to result in excellent electrochemical performance.

According to the above analysis (i.e., diminished parasitic reactions and thus improved reversibility), it is expected to reconstitute the interfacial H2O environment in BE when ZCDs are enriched on the region of Zn/H2O interface. DFT results present ZCDs’ higher affinity toward H2O regardless of the H2O structure, as the dominant hydroxyl and carboxyl (Fig. 4b) functional groups of ZCDs have stronger H2O-binding energy compared with Zn metal. Note that, the trace amounts of ZCDs and their large size (ca. 2.2 nm) rule out the possibility of physically blocking interfacial H2O in electrical double layer region. Impressively, the dispersed ZCDs in the BE have no obvious effects on pH, conductivity and solvated structure of the primary electrolyte (Figs. S11 and S12). As a result, we are able to focus on the effect of interfacial H2O environment evolution on the electrochemical performance of Zn anodes.

To verify our expectations, in situ ATR-SEIRAS [28] (Figs. 5 a, b and S13) was performed to compare the interface H2O evolution in BE and ISE, during the cathodic scan (−0.5 to −1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl) on the model Cu metal film surface, where the surface enhancement effect of ATR-SEIRA decays in very short distances (< 50 nm) and thus allows the ability to monitor interface H2O. Each positive going spectrum represents an increase in absorbance and vice versa. In BE, as the potential decreases, relative intensity of OH stretching band (3000 – 3600 cm−1) of H2O keeps increasing and gradually shifts to lower wavenumber. The O–H stretching band can be resolved into three types of O–H stretching vibrations corresponding to distinct interface H2O structures (Fig. 5c and d): symmetric H2O (at 3250 cm−1), asymmetric H2O (i.e., solvated H2O, at 3450 cm−1) and isolated H2O (at ∼3600 cm−1). Specifically, the ratio of symmetric H2O in BE increases to 24.3% (at −1.05 V, a Zn2+ deposition potential leaving a great deal of H2O molecules) and to 83.8% (at −1.2 V, Zn2+ deposition termination potential), asymmetric H2O decreased to 73.75% (at −1.05 V) and to 15.5% (at −1.2 V), and isolated H2O decreases to 1.93% (at −1.05 V) and to 0.6% (at −1.2 V) (Figs. 5e and S14). Conversely, the solvated H2O (at 3450 cm−1) has a significantly lower percentage (e.g., 59.65% in −1.05 V and 29.14% in −1.065 V) in ISE during whole cathodic scanning process and the isolated H2O completely disappears at −1.05 V. Moreover, a continued enrichment of ZCDs at the potential region from −0.75 to −1.135 V is also observed, as evidenced by the increasing intensity of C = C stretching (at 1591 cm−1). Note that, the negative charged ZCDs (zeta potential: −11.6 mV) start to desorb as the potential decrease to −1.2 V due to intense electrostatic repulsion. Consequently, it is reasonably sure that the ZCDs can dynamically enrich on the Zn anode surface and serve as the regulatory site of interface H2O structures.

So far, there has been considerable interest on the usage of inorganic materials that exhibit favorable dispersion in aqueous media, primarily for its ability to suppress dendrite growth [29–31]. Nevertheless, the link between dendrite formation, interfacial H2O structures, H2O-induced parasitic reactions has remained incompletely understood, owing to complexity of interfacial environments. In light of direct in situ spectroscopic evidence, the functional mechanisms of ZCDs are summarized as follows: (1) ZCDs can be dynamically enriched on the Zn anode surface during Zn2+ deposition potential region, which serve as a matrix for the regulation of interfacial H2O structures; (2) the H2O deprotonation and hydrolysis capacity decrease due to changes in structure of interfacial H2O [26,27,32] (as demonstrated by the ratio of solvated H2O decreasing in ISE compared to BE in Fig. 5e); Furthermore, the weakened H2O activity inhibits severe interfacial parasitic reactions and thus reduces deposition rate at any protrusion of electrified surface [33,34], facilitating homogeneous Zn2+ flux and reversible Zn2+ plating/stripping process. Consequently, these obtained results provide direct molecular evidence that the enhanced electrochemical performance of Zn anode can be attributed to the reduced reactive solvated H2O regulated by dynamically enriched ZCDs.

Finally, the function of ISE was also tested in hybrid supercapacitors in the voltage range of 0.2–1.8 V and full cells in 0.3–1.6 V by coupling with active carbon (AC) and commercial V2O5 cathodes, respectively. The rate tests of Zn||V2O5 cells and Zn||AC hybrid supercapacitors show a higher specific discharge capacity than that in the absence of ZCDs (Fig. 6 a and c), illustrating good compatibility between different systems and the modified electrolyte. When galvanostatically cycled at 3 A g − 1, the Zn||AC using ISE maintains ∼93% of initial highest capacity after 8000 cycles (Fig. 6b), whereas the reference cell survives only ∼5000 cycles owing to the aggravated formation of Zn corrosion and dendrite growth. Likewise, the two cells initially possess similar capacities at a current density of 3 A g − 1 (Fig. 6d), but the cell with BE exhibited the faster capacity degradation (with only ∼36% capacity retention) in the following cycles. In contrast, befitting from the regulated interfacial H2O microenvironment and the resultant diminished H2O-induced parasitic reactions, the full cell employing ISE delivers an impressive capacity retention of ∼83% over 4000 cycles, leading to a low decay rate of 0.0027 mAh g − 1 per cycle. Similar superiority also applies to cycling at low current density of 0.5 A/g (Fig. S15).

In summary, a H2O-regulating strategy via a ZCDs additive has been proposed to resolve the H2O-induced parasitic reactions (i.e., self-corrosion and HER), benefiting from which the Zn anode is endowed with suppressed HER and dendrite-free Zn plating/stripping, and substantially prolonged cycle life. Dynamic evolution of interfacial H2O molecular during Zn2+ depositing, realized through a combination of DFT calculations and in situ ATR-SEIRAS, is explored to correlates interfacial H2O structure with Zn anode stability, highlighting the importance of controlling the microenvironment of interfacial H2O at electrified Zn/electrolyte interface. The in-depth understanding of the interfacial microenvironment herein will provide insightful guidelines for designing next-generation aqueous Zn-based systems. Moreover, the system of in situ analyses implemented in this work should also serve as the paradigm for the future studies that aim at a fundamental understanding of the interfacial reactions in aqueous zinc-ion batteries and analogous systems.

Jie Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing-original draft. Lingyun Xiong: Formal analysis, Validation. Zhiwei Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing-review & editing. Zhi Yang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Yazhou Chen: Investigation, Software. Guiming Zhong: Investigation Limin Guo: Resources, Supervision, Validation. Zhangquan Peng: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22102183, 22202202, 22232005, 22242009), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M713091), National Key R&D Program of China (Project No. 2022YFA1504002), "Scientist Studio Funding" from Tianmu Lake Institute of Advanced Energy Storage Technologies Co., Ltd., and Dalian supports high-level talent innovation and entrepreneurship projects (no. 2021RD14).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2023.102904.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Renewable energy: back the renewables boom

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.E. Trancik, Renewable energy: back the renewables boom, Nature. 507 (2014) 300–302. https://doi.org/10.1038/507300a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Challenges and strategies for high-energy aqueous electrolyte rechargeable batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, X. Liu, H. Li, I. Hasa, S. Passerini, Challenges and strategies for high-energy aqueous electrolyte rechargeable batteries, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (2021) 598–616. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.202004433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Active materials for aqueous zinc ion batteries: synthesis, crystal structure, morphology, and electrochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jia, C. Liu, Z.G. Neale, J. Yang, G. Cao, Active materials for aqueous zinc ion batteries: synthesis, crystal structure, morphology, and electrochemistry, Chem. Rev. 120 (2020) 7795–7866. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00628.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Issues and opportunities facing aqueous zinc-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Tang, L. Shan, S. Liang, J. Zhou, Issues and opportunities facing aqueous zinc-ion batteries, Energy Environ. Sci. 12 (2019) 3288–3304. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9EE02526J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies for the Stabilization of Zn Metal Anodes for Zn-Ion Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Yi, G. Chen, F. Hou, L. Wang, J. Liang, Strategies for the Stabilization of Zn Metal Anodes for Zn-Ion Batteries, Adv. Energy Mater. 11 (2021) 2003065. https://doi.org/10.1002/aenm.202003065.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scientific challenges for the implementation of Zn-Ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.E. Blanc, D. Kundu, L.F. Nazar, Scientific challenges for the implementation of Zn-Ion batteries, Joule. 4 (2020) 771–799. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2020.03.002.
